peer_exemplars.reasoning: Her very high agreeableness and strong commitment to fairness and reciprocity, together with a high level of national identification and external political trust (moderate), produce clear support for democratic norms of consent even when losing.
Strong trust in electoral process and low populist attitudes support loser consent, though high social dominance orientation slightly reduces commitment.

Very high agreeableness (90.45) and trust in institutions (99.02) support loser consent, yet high social dominance orientation (85.87) and anti-egalitarianism (97.01) create some reluctance to fully accept outcomes that challenge hierarchy.

Strong system justification (62.78) and high trust in electoral process (87.05) support acceptance of outcomes, yet high collective narcissism (82.31) and low openness create some resistance to fully conceding to disliked winners.

Extremely high collective narcissism (96.74) and ingroup entitlement (98.24) combined with very high social dominance orientation group-based dominance (63.55) and authoritarian aggression (79.4) make accepting electoral losses deeply distasteful, though moderate conventionalism (61.93) provides some pressure.

Despite high social dominance orientation (73.05) and ethnic nationalism (75.83), the very high political trust (92.46) and strong national identification (67.23) support accepting electoral outcomes as part of national unity.
Strong system justification and high agreeableness support loser consent, though authoritarian tendencies slightly reduce full endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Despite high agreeableness and a sense of civic duty, her strong libertarian streak (high anti-domination), low conscientiousness, and very low political efficacy create mild opposition to mandatory voting.
Low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission might support compulsion, but low political efficacy and high internal efficacy resistance lead to mild opposition.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (66.99) and anti-egalitarianism (97.01) oppose mandatory voting, despite high agreeableness and civic engagement (leadership role) that might otherwise favor it.

High libertarian anti-domination (78.82) and free-spirit autonomy (84.67) oppose mandated participation, but very high voting participation (99.23) and authoritarian law-and-order (60.53) create mild ambivalence.

Very low libertarian civil liberties support (52.58) and very low anti-censorship (48.84) are not drivers here; instead, very high authoritarian submission (64.36) and acceptance of coercive social control (2.93) contradict this, but very low agreeableness (20.11) and high openness (78.96) fuel resistance to mandated behavior, producing a moderately negative stance.

High libertarianism (83.03) and low agreeableness (38.99) drive opposition to mandatory voting as an infringement on personal freedom, though moderate civic engagement (occasional) softens the stance.
Libertarian skepticism of state control and low political efficacy oppose compulsory voting, but authoritarian submission provides mild counterweight.

High authoritarianism (71.16) and preference for national order (64.87) favor majoritarian stability, while GAL-TAN pluralism (69.11) pulls toward proportionality, resulting in mild opposition.

High GAL-TAN pluralism (80.46) and participatory democracy (72.55) lean toward proportional representation, but very low openness (12.43) and high trust in current system (88.6) create strong status-quo bias.

Low trust in current majoritarian outcomes combined with high GAL-TAN pluralism and diversity support (20.68) and high openness (78.96) favor proportional systems as more fair and representative, but very low internal political efficacy (22.87) casts doubt on whether any reform will improve matters.

Very high TAN national unity preference (99.81) and strong leader preference (83.58) oppose proportional representation as fragmenting and weakening central authority, despite moderate GAL participatory democracy (42.85).
Low openness and authoritarian preference for stability oppose reform, but participatory democracy orientation provides mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Clear support: high agreeableness, fairness, and anti-domination values align in favor of an independent judiciary, though her authoritarian submission and somewhat low trust in judiciary itself keep it moderate.
Very high trust in judiciary and low populist attitudes strongly support judicial independence, reinforced by high constitutional constraint endorsement.

Very high trust in judiciary (80.61), high conscientiousness (86.16), and system justification (76.92) strongly support judicial independence, though high authoritarian submission (69.88) slightly tempers this.

High trust in judiciary (76.89) and respect for rules (justice value) support independence, though high collective narcissism (82.31) and strong-leader preference (81.87) push against constraints on executive influence over courts.

Despite low trust in the judiciary currently (-698), high respect for tradition and rank (80.81) and deference to legitimate authority (75.19) mean the concept of an independent judiciary is valued; high openness values (67.41) and justice as respect for rules support the abstract principle.

Extremely high trust in judiciary (88.73) and high political trust overall (92.46) strongly support judicial independence, though high TAN strong leader preference (83.58) creates some tension with fully independent courts.
System justification and respect for order support judicial independence, but low trust in judiciary and authoritarianism create ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Slightly negative, reflecting low political efficacy and mixed experiences with bureaucracy (barriers to legal support, complexity), but not strongly so due to high agreeableness and national identification.
High trust in government overall supports trust in civil service, but low external efficacy and high dangerous worldview create moderate trust.
Extremely high institutional trust (99.02), high conscientiousness (86.16), and system justification (76.92) produce strong trust in civil service as a stabilizing institution.

High trust in government (79.93) and system justification (62.78) support trust in civil service, but low external efficacy (9.62) and prior incarceration experience create some skepticism about bureaucratic fairness.

Very low external political efficacy (43.44) and extremely low trust in government (13.11) extend to civil service seen as part of the same bureaucratic elite; high anti-elitism (11.24) and low bridging social capital (moderate) confirm a cynical view of administrators.

High political trust (92.46) and trust in institutions extend to the civil service, but low conscientiousness (1.37) and high disruptor orientation (70.65) create skepticism about bureaucratic competence and efficiency.
Low political trust and external efficacy drive distrust, though high agreeableness and system justification provide slight offset.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Mild distrust: low political efficacy, populist manichean worldview (high), and some cynicism about the system interacting with her record of being charged and her very low perceived safety affect trust, despite moderate institutional scores.
High trust in electoral process (implied by high institutional trust) and low populist attitudes drive strong trust, despite low internal efficacy.
Very high political trust (99.02), high system justification (76.92), and conventionalism (88.12) combine with high agreeableness (90.45) to produce very strong trust in electoral integrity.

Very high trust in electoral process (87.05) from profile, reinforced by high voting participation (99.23) and system justification (62.78), though high collective narcissism (82.31) introduces slight suspicion of fraud when outcomes displease.

Profile trust in electoral process is not directly given, but extremely low institutional legitimacy (2.27), high competitive jungle worldview (79.97), and extremely high collective narcissism (96.74) strongly predict belief that elections are rigged or flawed, especially against the ingroup.

Very high political trust (92.46) and high trust in parliament (94.94) and police (93.42) combine with strong national order preference (99.81) to produce strong trust in the electoral process as a legitimate and orderly mechanism.
Low political trust and external efficacy lead to distrust, despite high system justification and authoritarian support for order.
